questions.stem: A 35-year-old builder presents with acute low back pain after lifting a heavy object. He describes a sharp pain radiating down the posterior aspect of his right thigh and calf, with mild numbness over the lateral border of his right foot. Straight leg raise is positive at 40° on the right. Neurological examination reveals a normal ankle jerk bilaterally. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The classic sciatica distribution (posterior thigh/calf), positive straight leg raise, and absence of vascular or inflammatory features point to lumbar disc herniation. The spared ankle jerk suggests L5/S1 disc herniation (S1 root) is less likely than L4/L5 disc herniation (L5 root) here, but the question tests diagnosis rather than level. Lumbar spinal stenosis typically causes neurogenic claudication, not acute radiculopathy.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old office worker presents with neck pain radiating down his left arm to the thumb and index finger. He reports pins and needles in the thumb and difficulty gripping objects. On examination, there is weakness of elbow flexion and wrist extension, and the biceps reflex is reduced. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The dermatomal distribution (thumb/index finger), weakness in C6-innervated muscles (biceps, brachioradialis), and reflex loss clearly indicate a cervical radiculopathy, most commonly due to disc herniation. Brachial neuritis typically presents with severe shoulder pain followed by patchy weakness, not a clear dermatomal pattern. Carpal tunnel syndrome affects the median nerve at the wrist, causing thenar weakness and numbness in the first three fingers but not arm or neck symptoms.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with sudden severe low back pain after a fall at home. She now reports numbness in both legs and around the buttocks, and has been unable to pass urine for several hours. On examination, anal sphincter tone is reduced. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Saddle anesthesia, urinary retention, and bilateral leg symptoms point to cauda equina syndrome, often caused by a large central disc herniation. Conus medullaris syndrome affects the spinal cord tip, causing early sphincter and sacral signs but less prominent leg pain. Acute disc herniation without cauda equina would not cause sphincter loss. Transverse myelitis is inflammatory and usually has a more gradual onset.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old man presents with a 3-month history of mid-back pain and a 'band-like' tightness around his chest. He has also noticed progressive stiffness and unsteadiness when walking. Cardiac investigations including ECG and troponin were normal. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Thoracic disc herniation is rare but presents with midline dorsal pain, radicular band-like pain (mimicking cardiac pain), and signs of myelopathy (leg stiffness, unsteadiness) due to spinal cord compression. Angina is cardiac and not associated with myelopathy. Herpes zoster would cause a rash and acute pain. Multiple sclerosis is relapsing-remitting. Spinal tumour is a differential but would usually show more progressive neurological loss.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old nurse presents with acute severe low back pain after transferring a patient. The next day she notices she cannot lift her right foot when walking. On examination, there is weakness of ankle dorsiflexion and numbness over the dorsum of the foot and lateral leg. The ankle jerk is normal. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Foot drop with L5 dermatomal sensory loss (lateral leg and dorsum of foot) and a normal ankle jerk (S1) localizes the lesion to the L5 nerve root, most commonly due to a posterolateral disc herniation at L4/L5. Common peroneal nerve palsy typically causes sensory loss over the dorsum of the foot but spares the lateral leg (supplied by L5 nerve root via lateral sural nerve) and is not associated with acute back pain. L5/S1 disc herniation affects the S1 root, impairing ankle jerk and plantar flexion.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old man presents with a 4-week history of right-sided sciatica and paraesthesia in the L5 dermatome. He has no red flags. What is the single most appropriate investigation to confirm the diagnosis?
questions.explanation: MRI is the gold standard for diagnosing lumbar disc herniation and nerve root compression, as it provides detailed soft tissue resolution. Plain X-ray does not visualise discs, CT gives less detail of nerve roots, and NCS/EMG are not first-line. Bone scan is for infection/tumour.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old woman presents with acute onset of bilateral leg weakness, perineal numbness, and urinary retention. She has a history of previous lumbar disc prolapse. Which investigation is most appropriate urgently?
questions.explanation: Cauda equina syndrome is a neurosurgical emergency requiring urgent MRI to assess for compression. MRI is preferred over CT for soft tissue. CT myelogram is an alternative if MRI contraindicated, but here MRI is first line. EMG is not diagnostic.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man with known L4/5 disc herniation on MRI 6 months ago now presents with new right foot drop. Which investigation is most appropriate to assess the current nerve root involvement?
options: Nerve conduction studies of the common peroneal nerve
questions.explanation: MRI is the best modality to reassess disc herniation and nerve root compression. NCS can help localise but are not first-line for structural assessment. CT and X-ray are inferior.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old man presents with neck pain and progressive clumsiness of his hands, along with a Lhermitte sign. He has no contraindications to MRI. What is the most appropriate investigation?
questions.explanation: MRI is the investigation of choice for cervical disc herniation and myelopathy, providing excellent visualisation of the spinal cord and discs. CT is for bony detail, X-ray only for alignment, EMG for neuropathy, Doppler for vascular.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man with a permanent pacemaker presents with severe sciatica and a positive straight leg raise test. He cannot have an MRI. What is the single most appropriate investigation to confirm the diagnosis of lumbar disc herniation?
options: CT lumbar spine with intravenous contrast
questions.explanation: When MRI is contraindicated, CT myelogram (CT after intrathecal contrast) is the best alternative to visualise nerve root compression. CT without contrast is less sensitive for disc herniation. X-ray and bone scan are not diagnostic. Ultrasound is limited.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old office worker presents with acute low back pain and right-sided sciatica for 2 weeks. Examination reveals lumbar flexion limitation, positive straight leg raise at 40°, and no motor or sensory deficit. MRI confirms a small L4/L5 disc protrusion without nerve root compression. What is the most appropriate first-line management?
options: Prescribe ibuprofen and refer for physiotherapy
Advise bed rest for 48 hours and prescribe diazepam
Prescribe oral morphine and arrange nerve root block
questions.explanation: NICE guidelines advise conservative management for acute sciatica without red flags or significant neurological deficit. Bed rest is discouraged; early mobilization and NSAIDs (e.g., ibuprofen) with physiotherapy are first-line. Surgical referral is reserved for severe or progressive deficits.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old builder presents with severe left sciatica and foot drop (ankle dorsiflexion 2/5) for 2 days. MRI shows a large L4/L5 disc extrusion compressing the L5 nerve root. He has no bowel/bladder symptoms. What is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Prescribe strong opioids and arrange outpatient physiotherapy
Perform a nerve root block under fluoroscopy
Start oral gabapentin and observe for 6 weeks
questions.explanation: A motor deficit (foot drop) is an indication for urgent surgical referral—discectomy within days to weeks to prevent permanent weakness. Steroids or nerve blocks do not address the mechanical compression. Conservative management is inappropriate given the acute, severe motor loss.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old woman with known L4/L5 disc herniation presents with acute urinary retention, bilateral leg numbness, and loss of anal sphincter tone. MRI confirms cauda equina syndrome. What is the most appropriate immediate management?
options: Prescribe oral codeine and arrange physiotherapy
Insert a urinary catheter and schedule MRI in 24 hours
Refer for urgent nerve conduction studies
questions.explanation: Cauda equina syndrome is a surgical emergency requiring decompression within 24-48 hours to prevent permanent paraplegia and incontinence. Corticosteroids, catheterization alone, or investigations are temporizing but not definitive; surgery is the priority.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old nurse presents with neck pain and right arm paraesthesia radiating to the thumb for 3 weeks. Examination shows mild C6 root weakness (deltoid/biceps 4/5) and reduced biceps jerk. MRI reveals a small C5/C6 disc protrusion. What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
options: Hard cervical collar immobilisation for 6 weeks
questions.explanation: For mild cervical radiculopathy without myelopathy, conservative management with NSAIDs (e.g., naproxen) and physiotherapy is first-line. Surgery is reserved for progressive weakness or failure of conservative treatment. Collar immobilisation is not routinely recommended; early mobilisation is preferred.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman at 32 weeks gestation presents with acute low back pain and right sciatica. Examination reveals no motor deficit, straight leg raise positive at 50°. MRI (performed with fetal shielding) shows a small L5/S1 disc herniation. Which analgesic is contraindicated as first-line treatment?
questions.explanation: Ibuprofen (NSAID) is contraindicated in the third trimester due to risks of premature closure of the ductus arteriosus, oligohydramnios, and fetal renal impairment. Paracetamol is first-line. Opioids like codeine may be used cautiously but NSAIDs are avoided.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old warehouse worker presents with acute low back pain and right leg pain (dermatomal L5 distribution) for 2 days. Straight leg raise is positive on the right at 40°. Neurological examination is normal. No red flags (no fever, no saddle anaesthesia, no urinary retention). What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Advise rest, avoid activities, and take regular paracetamol and ibuprofen
questions.explanation: NICE guidelines advise conservative management for acute sciatica without red flags. Initial treatment includes analgesia and activity modification. MRI is not indicated unless red flags are present or symptoms persist beyond 6–8 weeks.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old teacher presents with left leg pain (dermatomal L4 distribution) for 6 weeks. She has no motor deficit, no sensory loss, and no red flags. An MRI performed last week shows a left L4/L5 disc herniation. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Continue conservative management with analgesia and physiotherapy
questions.explanation: Most disc herniations improve with conservative management. Without cauda equina syndrome or progressive motor deficit, 6–8 weeks of conservative care is first line. Surgery is considered if symptoms persist or worsen.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man presents to the emergency department with sudden-onset bilateral leg pain, numbness in the 'saddle' area, and difficulty passing urine for the past 6 hours. On examination, he has reduced anal sphincter tone and a palpable bladder. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Call neurosurgery for immediate surgical decompression
questions.explanation: Suspect cauda equina syndrome. Urgent MRI (within 1–2 hours) is required to confirm the diagnosis and guide surgical decompression. Catheterisation may be needed but is not the first step; imaging must precede surgery.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old woman with known L5/S1 disc herniation (diagnosed 3 months ago) presents with progressive foot drop on the left over the past week. On examination, she has MRC grade 3/5 power in ankle dorsiflexion and great toe extension. She has no saddle anaesthesia or urinary symptoms. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Prescribe a course of oral corticosteroids
Refer for urgent neurosurgical opinion (within 24-48 hours)
Arrange physiotherapy to strengthen ankle dorsiflexors
Repeat MRI in 6 weeks to reassess
questions.explanation: Progressive motor deficit (foot drop) is an indication for urgent surgical referral. Steroids are not recommended; physiotherapy is not first-line for progressive weakness.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman who is 30 weeks pregnant presents with acute right leg pain (dermatomal S1 distribution) shooting down to the foot. Straight leg raise is positive. No red flags. She is otherwise healthy. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Prescribe ibuprofen 400 mg three times daily
Advise acetaminophen (paracetamol) regularly and apply heat to the back
questions.explanation: In pregnancy, NSAIDs are avoided in the third trimester. X-ray is contraindicated. MRI is not needed without red flags. Safe analgesia is paracetamol and non-pharmacological measures.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old presents with acute low back pain and left sided sciatica. On examination, straight leg raise is positive on the left. Which single finding would prompt urgent spinal imaging?
questions.explanation: Saddle anaesthesia is a red flag for cauda equina syndrome requiring emergency MRI, unlike isolated motor or reflex changes.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old presents with neck pain and clumsiness of the hands. On examination, there is weakness of the left triceps and hyperreflexia in both lower limbs. Which clinical sign confirms a diagnosis of cervical myelopathy?
questions.explanation: Babinski sign indicates an upper motor neuron lesion, confirming myelopathy; other signs may suggest but do not confirm cord compression.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old presents with severe low back pain and bilateral leg pain. He also reports night sweats and chills. Temperature is 38.5°C. Which finding is most concerning for a life-threatening mimic of disc herniation?
questions.explanation: Fever suggests spinal infection (e.g., epidural abscess), which can cause rapid cord compression and is a red flag distinct from typical disc herniation.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old presents with gradually worsening low back pain over 3 months, with nocturnal pain and weight loss of 5 kg. He has no radicular symptoms. Which clinical feature is a red flag for an alternative serious diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Night pain and weight loss are red flags for spinal malignancy (e.g., metastasis), prompting further imaging; disc herniation typically does not cause night pain.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old on warfarin for atrial fibrillation presents with sudden onset of severe back pain and rapidly progressive bilateral leg weakness over 6 hours. Which diagnosis must be urgently excluded?
options: Cauda equina syndrome from disc herniation
questions.explanation: Rapid onset while anticoagulated is classic for spinal epidural haematoma, requiring emergency decompression; other causes are less likely given the acuity and anticoagulation.
